paper_title: Carbon and nitrogen in bone collagen

methods.method_name: Bone Sample Selection and Initial Preparation
methods.description: Process for selecting archaeological bone samples from specific sites and periods, identifying the animal taxa, applying sampling strategies like Minimum Number of Individuals (MNI), and performing initial cleaning to remove surface contamination.

steps.description: Bone collagen samples were selected from a number of late Mesolithic and early Neolithic archaeological sites in southern Scandinavia (Table 1; Figure 1). Samples were taken from several transitional Mesolithic-early Neolithic sites as well as a few purely early Neolithic or late Mesolithic localities (Table 1; Figure 1).
steps.searchable_summary: select archaeological bone samples from specified sites and time periods
outputs: selected bone samples with site/period context

steps.description: Several taxa were sampled: red deer (Cervus elaphus), roe deer (Capreolus capreolus), domestic cattle and undifferentiated bovids (Bos sp.), which could be either domestic cattle or wild aurochs (Bos primigenius). Cattle from Scania were all considered domestic on the grounds that aurochs were absent in the region by the late Mesolithic (Aaris- Sørensen, 1999;Ekström, 1993). Domestic cattle from Jutland were only assigned to taxon if diagnostic measurements could be taken (Degerbøl and Fredskild, 1970). Otherwise, they were classified as Bos sp., given the possibility that they could be aurochs. Limited numbers of domestic ovicaprids (Capra sp./Ovis sp.) were also sampled but could not be differentiated to genus on morphological grounds.
steps.searchable_summary: identify and classify animal taxa for selected bone samples
outputs: bone samples with assigned taxa (Cervus elaphus, Capreolus capreolus, Bos sp., Bos primigenius, Capra sp./Ovis sp.)

steps.description: Wherever possible, a Minimum Number of Individuals (MNI) (Casteel and Grayson, 1977)-based sampling approach was taken at each site. However, in many cases, this simply was not possible, as much of the material derives from shell middens characterized by high fragmentation.
steps.searchable_summary: apply Minimum Number of Individuals (MNI) sampling strategy where feasible
inputs: selected bone samples from a site
outputs: subset of samples selected based on MNI criteria

steps.description: To mitigate the possibility of occasional accidental sampling from different elements from the same dead animal, a number of archaeological sites were sampled, and if possible remains from different features were selected.
steps.searchable_summary: select samples from different archaeological features within a site if possible
inputs: selected bone samples from a site
outputs: subset of samples selected from distinct features

steps.description: Several solely Mesolithic sites were included to ensure that the observed dietary values of wild species not the result of already-present human modification of the landscape.
steps.searchable_summary: include samples from solely Mesolithic sites in the selection
outputs: selected bone samples including those from solely Mesolithic sites

steps.description: Bone was first cleaned of surface contamination using a diamond-tipped dental rotary drill.
steps.searchable_summary: clean bone surface contamination using a dental drill

methods.description: Process for extracting and purifying collagen from cleaned bone samples using standard laboratory methodologies, noting minor variations between laboratories.

steps.description: Some minor variation of collagen extraction method (e.g. coarse grinding of the bone versus coarse chips of bone being used) was introduced by the standard protocols of different laboratories being applied.
steps.searchable_summary: perform initial physical preparation of bone (coarse grinding or using coarse chips)
outputs: physically prepared bone material (ground or chipped)

steps.description: Nevertheless, all collagen purification followed standard extraction methodology (Ambrose and DeNiro, 1986;Brown et al., 1988;DeNiro, 1985;Longin, 1971).
steps.searchable_summary: perform collagen extraction and purification using standard methodology

methods.method_name: Stable Isotope Analysis and Data Filtering
methods.description: Analysis of purified bone collagen for stable carbon (δ13C) and nitrogen (δ15N) isotope ratios, including quality control procedures and filtering of results based on the atomic carbon to nitrogen (C:N) ratio to assess diagenesis.

steps.description: Samples were analysed at the University of Waterloo's Stable Isotope Facility and the University of Bradford's Archaeological Stable Isotope facility and a few at the CHRONO Centre at Queen's University Belfast.
steps.searchable_summary: analyze purified collagen samples for δ13C and δ15N stable isotope ratios

steps.description: All laboratories strictly adhere to rigorous international quality control guidelines through the application of international reference standards.
steps.searchable_summary: apply international quality control guidelines and reference standards during isotope analysis
outputs: isotope data adhering to quality standards

steps.description: Only data with atomic carbon to nitrogen ratios in the range 2.9-3.6, indicating low probability of diagenesis (DeNiro, 1985), are interpreted.
steps.searchable_summary: filter isotope data based on atomic C:N ratio (2.9-3.6)
inputs: raw stable isotope data (including C:N ratio)

steps.description: Maximum analytical error reported among the three labs was 0.25‰ for δ 13 C and 0.3‰ for δ 15 N, well within the reported range of variation in the data.
steps.searchable_summary: record and report maximum analytical error for δ13C and δ15N measurements
